evidence_quotes.quote: The issue is that the purchase is often a precarious form of renting, not true ownership.
When you interact with one online service, your personal data and behavior can be collected and then shared or sold to other companies.
This allows a separate, seemingly unrelated service to build a profile on you before you even create an account.
but to use them, you must pay a monthly subscription fee.
This turns a one-time purchase into a continuous rental.

paragraphs.text: Digital purchases aren't what they seem.We "buy" apps, movies, books, and even games, but our access can disappear at any time.The issue is that the purchase is often a precarious form of renting, not true ownership.This is a problem that the "Stop Killing Games" initiative has brought to the forefront, as it advocates for consumer rights and the preservation of games that are rendered unplayable when a publisher decides to pull the plug.In today's digital world, a few powerful companies act as gatekeepers to information and commerce, creating a concentration of power that leads to censorship and significant privacy concerns.For example, infrastructure providers like Cloudflare and financial services like Visa and PayPal have shown a willingness to block access to content and income streams for platforms like WikiLeaks, Aellune, OnlyFans, DLsite, Melonbooks, Niconico, Pornhub, Fantia, Fanza, pictSPACE, Itch.io, DL.Getchu, Steam, Toranoana, Denpasoft, Pixiv, Patreon and others, creating a form of financial censorship that can silence independent creators and alternative voices.This issue is worsened by the reliance on cloud services and the demand for personal data.When companies control the servers for products, from software to appliances, they can revoke access at any time. This shifts the user-company relationship from one of ownership to a precarious form of renting.Additionally, many online services force users to share personal data or even government IDs to access basic functions. This extensive data collection creates a central point of failure, making users vulnerable to data breaches, identity theft, and unauthorized surveillance.The risk of storing all this personal data is high, as even institutional databases have proven to be vulnerable to hacking.These problems stem from a core issue: the concentration of power in a few corporate hands.To protect digital freedom, stronger safeguards are needed.This includes regulations that prevent platforms from blocking users without clear legal justification, ensuring personal data is stored securely, and allowing users to access services without compromising their privacy.We must advocate for laws that prioritize user rights over corporate profits to preserve our online freedom. In a world of extensive data tracking and sharing, a new form of discrimination has emerged.When you interact with one online service, your personal data and behavior can be collected and then shared or sold to other companies. This allows a separate, seemingly unrelated service to build a profile on you before you even create an account.This can lead to a denial of service or access; especially when you have to provide your ID in order to make an account or access to a site, app, server or a cloud service of any kind. This system of interconnected data creates a chilling effect on online behavior.Knowing that your actions on one platform could impact your access to services on another can make you hesitant to browse certain content or engage in specific activities, further eroding the promise of a free and open internet. In addition to the already mentioned problems, the trend of "smart" appliances, from cars to refrigerators and washing machines, is introducing a new problem: paying for functionality you already "own".This is a fundamental shift away from traditional consumer ownership. Manufacturers are building products with all their features already installed.For example, a car might have heated seats or a higher-performance engine mode already built in, but these are then locked behind a software paywall.You've paid for the physical components, but to use them, you must pay a monthly subscription fee.This turns a one-time purchase into a continuous rental.It's very similar to what is happening in the game industry since decades, but now it's affecting physical products necessary to live and work.You can't get groceries without a car, certain food goes bad quickly without a fridge and so on.
